expectations.text: No unexplained advanced git terms (rebase, HEAD, SHA, hash, commit used without kid-friendly definition)
expectations.evidence: All git concepts are explained in plain language. 'Git is a tool that helps people work on the same project together, kind of like a shared notebook.' No unexplained jargon.

expectations.text: Uses a relatable analogy for a 10-11 year old (school project, shared Google Doc, group assignment)
expectations.evidence: Uses group project/poster analogy and shared story writing analogy — both directly relatable to 5th graders.

expectations.text: Explanation follows a logical, sequential flow that builds understanding progressively
expectations.evidence: Clear progression: what is a conflict → analogy → how it happens step by step → what it looks like → how to fix it → why it matters.

expectations.text: Language and vocabulary are appropriate for a 5th grader (age 10-11), not too babyish, not too advanced
expectations.evidence: Perfect grade level. Uses 'version', 'resolved' (appropriate for 5th grade) without being overly simple. Doesn't say 'super duper' or use baby talk.
